1164	\begin{lemma}[Triple-transition bridge to \texorpdfstring{$\Theta_{IJ}$}{Theta_IJ}]
1165	  \label{lem:gr_baseChange_bridge_transition}
1166	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.baseChange_bridge_transition}
1167	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data, def:gr_chart_transition', def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij,
1168	    def:gr_away_pullback_iso, def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv,
1169	    lem:gr_baseChange_bridge_gammaSpec}
1170	  With the notation of \cref{lem:gr_baseChange_bridge_left}, let
1171	  \(t'_{IJK} : V_{IJK} \to U^J_K \times_{U^J} U^J_I\) be the triple transition of the glue datum
1172	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_transition'}). Through the source and target away-pullback identifications
1173	  (\cref{def:gr_away_pullback_iso}) and the order-swap isomorphism
1174	  (\cref{def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv}), \(t'_{IJK}\) is the \(\Spec\) morphism of the localised
1175	  triple-overlap transition \(\Theta_{IJ} : S_J \to S_I\) (\cref{def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij},
1176	  composed with the order-swap ring isomorphism). Consequently the global-sections base-change
1177	  map of \(t'_{IJK}\), transported through the affine global-sections identification, equals the
1178	  ring homomorphism \(\Theta_{IJ}\) (up to the order-swap); in particular the entrywise image of
1179	  the Cramer inverse \((X^J_K)^{-1}\) under the geometric base-change agrees with its image under
1180	  \(\Theta_{IJ}\).
1181	\end{lemma}
1182	\begin{proof}
1183	  \uses{def:gr_chart_transition', def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij, def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv,
1184	    lem:gr_baseChange_bridge_gammaSpec}
1185	  By \cref{def:gr_chart_transition'} the triple transition \(t'_{IJK}\) is the composite of the
1186	  source away-pullback identification, \(\Spec\Theta_{IJ}\) (\cref{def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij}), the
1187	  \(\Spec\) of the order-swap isomorphism (\cref{def:gr_away_mul_comm_equiv}), and the inverse
1188	  target away-pullback identification. The charts and overlaps being affine, applying
1189	  \cref{lem:gr_baseChange_bridge_gammaSpec} to each \(\Spec\) factor identifies the global-sections
1190	  comorphism of \(t'_{IJK}\), through the affine global-sections identifications and the order-swap,
1191	  with the ring homomorphism \(\Theta_{IJ}\). The entrywise image of \((X^J_K)^{-1}\) under this
1192	  comorphism therefore agrees with its image under \(\Theta_{IJ}\).
1193	\end{proof}

1195	\begin{lemma}[Base-change bridge to the localised cocycle ring homomorphisms]
1196	  \label{lem:gr_baseChange_bridge}
1197	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.baseChange_bridge}
1198	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_the_glue_data,
1199	    lem:gr_bundleCocycle_matrix, def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij, def:gr_away_incl_left,
1200	    def:gr_away_incl_right, lem:gr_baseChange_bridge_left,
1201	    lem:gr_baseChange_bridge_right, lem:gr_baseChange_bridge_transition}
1202	  Fix indices \(I, J, K\) and the triple overlap \(V_{IJK}\) of the chart cover
1203	  \(\{U^I\}\) of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}, \cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}).
1204	  The scheme-pullback base-change map on global sections
1205	  \(\Gamma(U^I_J, \mathcal{O}) \to \Gamma(V_{IJK}, \mathcal{O})\) induced by the first projection
1206	  \(p^{IJ}_{IJK}\), and likewise the maps induced by the second projection and by the triple
1207	  transition \(t'_{IJK}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}), are identified -- through the natural identification of global sections of an affine scheme
1208	  with its coordinate ring and the fact that the projections and triple transition are the
1209	  \(\Spec\) morphisms of the coordinate-ring homomorphisms by which the Grassmannian glue datum
1210	  (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}) is assembled -- with the ring homomorphisms
1211	  \(\mathrm{awayInclLeft}\) (\cref{def:gr_away_incl_left}),
1212	  \(\mathrm{awayInclRight}\) (\cref{def:gr_away_incl_right}) and \(\Theta_{IJ}\)
1213	  (\cref{def:gr_cocycle_theta_ij}) over which the matrix cocycle
1214	  \cref{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_matrix} is stated. Consequently the entrywise image of a Cramer
1215	  inverse \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) under the geometric base-change map agrees with its image under the
1216	  corresponding coordinate-ring homomorphism, so \cref{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_matrix} applies
1217	  to the base-changed matrices.
1218	\end{lemma}
1219	\begin{proof}
1220	  \uses{lem:gr_baseChange_bridge_left, lem:gr_baseChange_bridge_right,
1221	    lem:gr_baseChange_bridge_transition}
1222	  The three identifications are exactly the three projection bridges. The first projection is
1223	  handled by \cref{lem:gr_baseChange_bridge_left}, identifying its global-sections base-change
1224	  map with \(\mathrm{awayInclLeft}\); the second projection by
1225	  \cref{lem:gr_baseChange_bridge_right}, identifying it with \(\mathrm{awayInclRight}\); and the
1226	  triple transition \(t'_{IJK}\) by \cref{lem:gr_baseChange_bridge_transition}, identifying it
1227	  with \(\Theta_{IJ}\). Each bridge already records the agreement of the entrywise image of the
1228	  relevant Cramer inverse under the geometric base-change with its image under the named ring
1229	  homomorphism over which \cref{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_matrix} is stated, so assembling the three
1230	  gives the claim.
1231	\end{proof}

1233	\begin{lemma}[Transport and endpoint alignment of the bundle transitions]
1234	  \label{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_transport}
1235	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.bundleTransition_cocycle_transport}
1236	  \uses{def:gr_bundleTransition, lem:gr_bundleCocycle_matrix, lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_mul,
1237	    lem:gr_matrixEnd_pullback, lem:gr_baseChange_bridge,
1238	    lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport, lem:gr_glueData_bridges,
1239	    def:modules_pullbackComp, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
1240	  Fix indices \(I, J, K\) with triple overlap \(V_{IJK} = U^I_J \times_{U^I} U^I_K\),
1241	  and let \(\widehat{g}_{IJ}^{\,K}, \widehat{g}_{JK}^{\,I}, \widehat{g}_{IK}^{\,J}\) be
1242	  the base-change transports (\cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}) of the
1243	  three bundle transitions to \(V_{IJK}\). Under the free-pullback comparisons
1244	  (\cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}) and the endpoint bridges
1245	  (\cref{lem:gr_glueData_bridges}), each transported transition is identified with the
1246	  matrix automorphism of the corresponding base-changed Cramer inverse on the common
1247	  free sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{V_{IJK}}^{\,d}\); granting the matrix identity
1248	  \cref{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_matrix}, the composite satisfies
1249	  \[
1250	    \widehat{g}_{JK}^{\,I} \circ \widehat{g}_{IJ}^{\,K}
1251	      \;=\; \widehat{g}_{IK}^{\,J}
1252	  \]
1253	  as isomorphisms of sheaves of modules on \(V_{IJK}\). This is the substantive
1254	  transport step: the alignment of the three transports' domains and codomains.
1255	\end{lemma}
1256	\begin{proof}
1257	  \uses{def:gr_bundleTransition, lem:gr_bundleCocycle_matrix, lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_mul,
1258	    lem:gr_matrixEnd_pullback, lem:gr_baseChange_bridge,
1259	    lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport, lem:gr_glueData_bridges,
1260	    def:modules_pullbackComp, lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}
1261	  This is pure assembly of the two infrastructure pieces over the endpoint bridges. Each
1262	  bundle transition is, by \cref{def:gr_bundleTransition}, \(\mathrm{matrixToFreeIso}\) of a
1263	  localised Cramer inverse conjugated by the free-pullback comparisons
1264	  \cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}; its base-change transport
1265	  (\cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}) reassociates the iterated pullbacks via
1266	  \cref{def:modules_pullbackComp}, and the three endpoint bridges \cref{lem:gr_glueData_bridges}
1267	  pin the source, middle and target sheaves to a single \(\mathcal{O}_{V_{IJK}}^{\,d}\). By
1268	  \cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_pullback} each transport then equals \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}\) of the
1269	  base-changed Cramer inverse, and by \cref{lem:gr_baseChange_bridge} those base-changed matrices
1270	  are exactly \((X^I_J)^{-1}, (X^J_K)^{-1}, (X^I_K)^{-1}\) over the common triple-overlap ring of
1271	  \cref{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_matrix}. The linkage \cref{lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_mul} composes the
1272	  first two to \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}\bigl((X^J_K)^{-1}(X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr)\), and
1273	  by the matrix cocycle identity \cref{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_matrix} the argument equals
1274	  \((X^I_K)^{-1}\), so the composite equals \(\widehat{g}_{IK}^{\,J}\); the bridges make both
1275	  sides isomorphisms between the same pair of sheaves on \(V_{IJK}\).
1276	\end{proof}

1278	\begin{lemma}[Triple-overlap multiplicativity of the bundle transition (C2)]
1279	  \label{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_mul}
1280	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.bundleTransition_cocycle}
1281	  \uses{def:gr_bundleTransition, lem:gr_bundleCocycle_matrix, lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_mul,
1282	    lem:gr_bundleCocycle_transport, lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport,
1283	    lem:gr_glueData_bridges, def:modules_pullbackComp}
1284	  Over each triple overlap \(V_{IJK} = U^I_J \times_{U^I} U^I_K\) the base-change transports
1285	  (\cref{lem:modules_pullback_basechange_transport}) of the three bundle transitions satisfy
1286	  \[
1287	    \widehat{g}_{JK}^{\,I} \circ \widehat{g}_{IJ}^{\,K}
1288	      \;=\; \widehat{g}_{IK}^{\,J},
1289	  \]
1290	  the module-level form of the descent condition (C2) of \cref{def:scheme_modules_glue} for
1291	  the bundle data \((g_{I,J})\). This is the \(\mathrm{GL}_d\)-valued multiplicative cocycle
1292	  of \(\mathcal{U}\).
1293	\end{lemma}
1294	\begin{proof}
1295	  \uses{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_matrix, lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_mul,
1296	    lem:gr_bundleCocycle_transport}
1297	  The statement decomposes into three independent steps. First, the matrix-algebra core
1298	  \cref{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_matrix} establishes the Cramer-inverse cocycle
1299	  \((X^J_K)^{-1}(X^I_J)^{-1} = (X^I_K)^{-1}\) over the triple overlap. Second, the linkage
1300	  \cref{lem:gr_matrixToFreeIso_mul} records that composition of matrix automorphisms realises
1301	  matrix multiplication. Third, the transport step \cref{lem:gr_bundleCocycle_transport}
1302	  carries the three transitions to the common overlap \(V_{IJK}\), aligns their source,
1303	  middle, and target sheaves through the base-change transport and the endpoint bridges, and
1304	  -- feeding it the matrix identity through the linkage -- yields exactly the stated equality
1305	  \(\widehat{g}_{JK}^{\,I} \circ \widehat{g}_{IJ}^{\,K} = \widehat{g}_{IK}^{\,J}\).
1306	\end{proof}
1307	
1308	\begin{definition}
1309	
1310	[The universal quotient sheaf \(\mathcal{U}\)]
1311	  \label{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}
1312	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.universalQuotient}
1313	  \uses{def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_universalMinorInv, def:gr_transition,
1314	    lem:gr_cocycle, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:scheme_modules_glue,
1315	    def:gr_modules_glueRestrictionIso, def:gr_bundleTransition,
1316	    lem:gr_bundleCocycle_id, lem:gr_bundleCocycle_mul,
1317	    lem:gr_glueData_bridges, def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
1318	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
1319	  % sub-section "Universal quotient" (read from
1320	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L903-L910).
1321	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1322	  % "Compatible with the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$
1323	  %  for gluing the affine pieces $U^I$, we give gluing data
1324	  %  $(g_{I,J})$ for gluing together the trivial bundles $\oplus^d\,{\mathcal O}_{U^I}$
1325	  %  by putting
1326	  %  $$g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I_J)$$"
1327	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
1328	  Over each overlap \(U^I_J = \Spec R^I_J\) define the bundle-transition matrix
1329	  \[
1330	    g_{I,J} \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} \;\in\; \mathrm{GL}_d(R^I_J),
1331	  \]
1332	  the Cramer inverse of the localised \(J\)-minor (\cref{def:gr_universalMinorInv}),
1333	  which is invertible because \(\det(X^I_J)\) is a unit of \(R^I_J\). The matrices
1334	  \((g_{I,J})\) are compatible with the chart-gluing cocycle \((\theta_{I,J})\)
1335	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}, \cref{lem:gr_cocycle}): over a triple overlap they
1336	  satisfy \(g_{I,K} = g_{J,K}\, g_{I,J}\) and \(g_{I,I} = 1\), the multiplicative
1337	  cocycle condition for a vector bundle. Gluing the free rank-\(d\) sheaves
1338	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\) along the isomorphisms \(g_{I,J}\) over the cover
1339	  \(\{U^I\}\) of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) therefore
1340	  produces a locally free \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-module
1341	  \(\mathcal{U}\) of rank \(d\). Its determinant line bundle \(\det \mathcal{U}\)
1342	  has transition functions \(\det(g_{I,J}) = 1/P^I_J\), the data underlying the
1343	  Pl\"ucker embedding.
1344	\end{definition}
1345	
1346	The tautological quotient \(u\) is assembled from the chart quotients \(u^I\) by the
1347	section-level descent \cref{def:gr_modules_glueHom}; its one remaining content is the
1348	\emph{overlap-compatibility} of the family \((u^I)\) with the bundle gluing
1349	\((g_{I,J})\). The next four blocks isolate that compatibility: a rectangular analogue of
1350	the square matrix-endomorphism API (\cref{def:gr_matrixEnd},
1351	\cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_pullback}, \cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp}) for the
1352	\(r \to d\) chart quotient (\cref{def:gr_matrixEndRect},
1353	\cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_pullback}, \cref{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_comp}), the
1354	adjunction transposition that turns the descent condition into a pullback-level identity
1355	(\cref{lem:gr_tautologicalQuotientComponent_transpose}), and the matrix-core assembly
1356	that closes it (\cref{lem:gr_tautologicalQuotient_overlap}).
1357	
1358	\subsection{Matrix-calculus toolbox}
1359	
1360	The chart-cover lemma (\cref{lem:chartLocus_isOpenCover}) and the overlap compatibility of
1361	\cref{def:grPointOfRankQuotient} both rest on a small calculus of \emph{rectangular} matrix
1362	homomorphisms of free sheaves, generalising the square API
1363	(\cref{def:gr_matrixEnd}, \cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_comp}, \cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_one}). The
1364	blocks below collect that toolbox: the bridge to the square case, the identity and
1365	composition laws, injectivity of the matrix presentation, the column-restriction law, the
1366	affine splitting of epimorphisms of free sheaves and the resulting matrix right inverse, the
1367	field-level minor extraction, and the presented-matrix machinery that packages a quotient as
1368	a matrix along a morphism and proves Nitsure's change-of-basis identity. They are
1369	project-bespoke infrastructure.
1370	
1371	\begin{lemma}[Square and rectangular matrix homomorphisms coincide]
1372	  \label{lem:gr_matrixEnd_eq_matrixEndRect}
1373	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixEnd_eq_matrixEndRect}
1374	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEnd, def:gr_matrixEndRect}
1375	  For a square matrix \(M \in \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times d}(\Gamma(S,\mathcal{O}_S))\), the
1376	  square and rectangular matrix homomorphisms agree:
1377	  \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(M) = \mathrm{matrixEndRect}(M)\).
1378	\end{lemma}
1379	\begin{proof}
1380	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEnd, def:gr_matrixEndRect}
1381	  Both are the morphism assembled from the same biproduct components
1382	  \(\mathrm{scalarEnd}(M_{k,\ell})\) over the index set \(\{1,\dots,d\}\); the two
1383	  constructions are literally the same, so the equality holds by definition.
1384	\end{proof}
1385	
1386	\begin{lemma}[Identity law for \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}\)]
1387	  \label{lem:gr_matrixEndRect_one}
1388	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.matrixEndRect_one}
1389	  \uses{def:gr_matrixEndRect, lem:gr_matrixEnd_one, lem:gr_matrixEnd_eq_matrixEndRect}
1390	  The rectangular homomorphism of the identity matrix is the identity morphism:
1391	  \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(1) = \mathrm{id}_{\mathcal{O}_S^{\,d}}\).
1392	\end{lemma}
1393	\begin{proof}
1394	  \uses{lem:gr_matrixEnd_one, lem:gr_matrixEnd_eq_matrixEndRect}
1395	  By \cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_eq_matrixEndRect} the left side equals \(\mathrm{matrixEnd}(1)\),
1396	  which is the identity by \cref{lem:gr_matrixEnd_one}.
1397	\end{proof}

2100	  The \emph{Grassmannian functor}
2101	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d) = \Quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}
2102	  \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/\mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) is the contravariant functor
2103	  from schemes to sets defined as follows. For a scheme \(T\), a \emph{family of
2104	  rank-\(d\) quotients of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)} is a pair
2105	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) consisting of a locally free
2106	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-module \(\mathcal{F}\) of rank \(d\) together with a surjective
2107	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-linear homomorphism \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
2108	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\). Two such families
2109	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\) and \(\langle \mathcal{F}', q' \rangle\) are
2110	  \emph{equivalent} if there is an isomorphism \(f : \mathcal{F}
2111	  \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{F}'\) with \(f \circ q = q'\); equivalently
2112	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\) as subsheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\). Set
2113	  \[
2114	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)
2115	      \;=\; \bigl\{\, \text{equivalence classes } \langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle
2116	        \text{ of rank-}d\text{ quotients of } \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \,\bigr\}.
2117	  \]
2118	  For a morphism \(\varphi : T' \to T\), the map
2119	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(\varphi)\) sends \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle\)
2120	  to the pulled-back quotient \(\langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{F},\, \varphi^{*}q
2121	  \rangle\); this is well-defined on equivalence classes because pullback is right
2122	  exact and preserves local freeness of rank \(d\). (As \(\mathcal{F}\) is locally
2123	  free of rank \(d\), surjectivity is preserved and the support is automatically
2124	  proper -- all of \(T'\) -- so this is the special case
2125	  \(E = \mathcal{O}^{r}\), \(X = S = \operatorname{base}\) of the general Quot
2126	  functor of \cref{def:quot_functor}.)
2127	\end{definition}
2128	
2129	\section{The universal property}
2130	\label{sec:grquot_universal}
2131	
2132	The representability theorem \cref{thm:grassmannian_universal_property} has two halves:
2133	the forward map \(\varphi \mapsto \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u\rangle\)
2134	and its inverse, which manufactures a morphism \(T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) out of a rank-\(d\)
2135	quotient \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) on \(T\). The forward map needs the tautological
2136	quotient to actually be a quotient -- that \(u\) is an epimorphism
2137	(\cref{lem:tautologicalQuotient_epi}). The inverse is built Zariski-locally and glued: for
2138	each size-\(d\) subset \(I\) one isolates the open locus \(T_I \subseteq T\) where the
2139	\(I\)-indexed columns of \(q\) form a basis of \(\mathcal{F}\), classifies \(T_I \to U^I\)
2140	there, and glues over the cover \(\{T_I\}\). The blocks of this section supply the four
2141	ingredients of that inverse: the iso-locus of a sheaf morphism (\cref{def:isoLocus}) and
2142	the fact that a morphism becomes invertible after restriction to its iso-locus
2143	(\cref{lem:isIso_pullback_isoLocus_map}); the chart locus \(T_I\) (\cref{def:chartLocus})
2144	and the proof that the chart loci cover \(T\) (\cref{lem:chartLocus_isOpenCover}); and the
2145	glued classifying morphism itself (\cref{def:grPointOfRankQuotient}).
2146	
2147	\begin{lemma}[The tautological quotient is an epimorphism]
2148	  \label{lem:tautologicalQuotient_epi}
2149	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.tautologicalQuotient_epi}
2150	  \uses{lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_epi, def:tautological_quotient}
2151	  The tautological quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\,r}
2152	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) (\cref{def:tautological_quotient}) is an epimorphism of
2153	  sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}\)-modules.
2154	\end{lemma}
2155	\begin{proof}
2156	  \uses{lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_epi, def:tautological_quotient}
2157	  Being an epimorphism of sheaves of modules is local on the base: a morphism is epi
2158	  precisely when its restriction to every member of an open cover is epi, since the
2159	  cokernel is computed chartwise and a sheaf vanishing on a cover vanishes. The charts
2160	  \(\{U^I\}\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and over \(U^I\) the tautological quotient,
2161	  transported through the trivialisation \(\mathcal{U}|_{U^I} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\)
2162	  of \cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}, is the chart quotient \(u^I :
2163	  \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d}\) (\cref{def:tautological_quotient}).
2164	  Each \(u^I\) is a split epimorphism: its \(I\)-minor is the identity \(1_{d\times d}\),
2165	  so the coordinate inclusion \(\mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,d} \hookrightarrow
2166	  \mathcal{O}_{U^I}^{\,r}\) on the \(I\)-columns is a section of \(u^I\), whence \(u^I\) is
2167	  epi (\cref{lem:gr_chartQuotientMap_epi}). As \(u^I\) is epi on every chart, \(u\) is epi.
2168	\end{proof}

2233	\begin{definition}[Iso-locus of a morphism of sheaves of modules]
2234	  \label{def:isoLocus}
2235	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isoLocus}
2236	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) a morphism of sheaves
2237	  of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The \emph{iso-locus} \(\mathrm{isoLocus}(\varphi) \subseteq X\)
2238	  is the open subscheme
2239	  \[
2240	    \mathrm{isoLocus}(\varphi)
2241	      \;=\; \bigsqcup\,\bigl\{\, U \subseteq X \text{ open} \;:\;
2242	        \varphi|_U := (\iota_U^{*}\varphi) \text{ is an isomorphism} \,\bigr\},
2243	  \]
2244	  the supremum (union) of all opens \(U\) over which the pullback \(\iota_U^{*}\varphi\) of
2245	  \(\varphi\) to the open subscheme \(U\) is an isomorphism of sheaves of
2246	  \(\mathcal{O}_U\)-modules. Equivalently, a point \(t \in X\) lies in
2247	  \(\mathrm{isoLocus}(\varphi)\) iff \(\varphi\) restricts to an isomorphism over some open
2248	  neighbourhood of \(t\). It is the largest open on which \(\varphi\) is invertible; Mathlib
2249	  has no iso-locus primitive for morphisms of sheaves of modules, so it is set up directly
2250	  as this supremum.
2251	\end{definition}

2253	\begin{lemma}[Stalk-wise iso criterion]
2254	  \label{lem:isIso_of_stalkFunctor_map_iso_mathlib}
2255	  \lean{TopCat.Presheaf.isIso_of_stalkFunctor_map_iso}
2256	  \mathlibok
2257	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
2258	  Let \(\mathcal{F}, \mathcal{G}\) be sheaves on a topological space \(X\) valued in a
2259	  concrete category whose forgetful functor reflects isomorphisms and preserves limits and
2260	  filtered colimits, and let \(f : \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{G}\) be a morphism. If the
2261	  induced map on stalks \(f_t : \mathcal{F}_t \to \mathcal{G}_t\) is an isomorphism for every
2262	  point \(t \in X\), then \(f\) is an isomorphism.
2263	\end{lemma}

2265	\begin{lemma}[A morphism is invertible over its iso-locus]
2266	  \label{lem:isIso_pullback_isoLocus_map}
2267	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isIso_pullback_isoLocus_map}
2268	  \uses{def:isoLocus, lem:isIso_of_stalkFunctor_map_iso_mathlib}
2269	  For a morphism \(\varphi : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) of sheaves of
2270	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, the pullback of \(\varphi\) to the iso-locus
2271	  (\cref{def:isoLocus}),
2272	  \[
2273	    (\iota_{\mathrm{isoLocus}(\varphi)})^{*}\varphi
2274	      \;:\;
2275	    (\iota_{\mathrm{isoLocus}(\varphi)})^{*}\mathcal{M}
2276	      \;\longrightarrow\;
2277	    (\iota_{\mathrm{isoLocus}(\varphi)})^{*}\mathcal{N},
2278	  \]
2279	  is an isomorphism.
2280	\end{lemma}
2281	\begin{proof}
2282	  \uses{def:isoLocus, lem:isIso_of_stalkFunctor_map_iso_mathlib}
2283	  Being an isomorphism is a stalk-local property: by the stalk-wise criterion
2284	  (\cref{lem:isIso_of_stalkFunctor_map_iso_mathlib}) it suffices to show that the pulled-back
2285	  morphism induces an isomorphism on the stalk at every point \(t \in
2286	  \mathrm{isoLocus}(\varphi)\). By the definition of the iso-locus as a union of opens on
2287	  which \(\varphi\) restricts to an isomorphism (\cref{def:isoLocus}), each such \(t\) has an
2288	  open neighbourhood \(U \subseteq \mathrm{isoLocus}(\varphi)\) with \(\iota_U^{*}\varphi\) an
2289	  isomorphism; restriction of an isomorphism is an isomorphism, and pullback commutes with
2290	  taking stalks, so the stalk of the morphism at \(t\) coincides with the stalk of
2291	  \(\iota_U^{*}\varphi\) at \(t\), which is invertible. Hence every stalk map is an
2292	  isomorphism, and the criterion concludes that the global pullback over
2293	  \(\mathrm{isoLocus}(\varphi)\) is an isomorphism.
2294	\end{proof}

2315	\begin{definition}[Chart locus of a rank-\(d\) quotient]
2316	  \label{def:chartLocus}
2317	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartLocus}
2318	  \uses{def:isoLocus, def:gr_chartComposite, def:gr_rankQuotient}
2319	  Let \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) be a rank-\(d\) quotient of
2320	  \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\) on a scheme \(T\) (\cref{def:gr_rankQuotient}) and \(I \subseteq
2321	  \{1,\dots,r\}\) a size-\(d\) subset. The \emph{chart composite} is the morphism of sheaves
2322	  of modules
2323	  \[
2324	    s_I \circ q
2325	      \;:\;
2326	    \mathcal{O}_T^{\,d}
2327	      \overset{\ s_I\ }{\hookrightarrow} \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
2328	      \xrightarrow{\ q\ } \mathcal{F},
2329	  \]
2330	  obtained by precomposing the quotient \(q\) with the inclusion \(s_I\) of the
2331	  \(I\)-indexed coordinates of the trivial bundle. The \emph{chart locus}
2332	  \(T_I = \mathrm{chartLocus}(x, I) \subseteq T\) is the iso-locus
2333	  (\cref{def:isoLocus}) of this chart composite:
2334	  \[
2335	    T_I \;=\; \mathrm{isoLocus}(s_I \circ q),
2336	  \]
2337	  the largest open of \(T\) over which the \(I\)-columns of \(q\) form a basis of
2338	  \(\mathcal{F}\) -- i.e.\ over which \(s_I \circ q\) is an isomorphism. Over \(T_I\) the
2339	  quotient \(q\) is therefore presented, after trivialising \(\mathcal{F}\) by
2340	  \((s_I \circ q)^{-1}\), by a \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(I\)-minor is the identity,
2341	  exactly the shape of the universal matrix \(X^I\) realising the chart quotient \(u^I\)
2342	  (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}).
2343	\end{definition}

2345	\begin{lemma}[The chart loci cover \(T\)]
2346	  \label{lem:chartLocus_isOpenCover}
2347	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartLocus_isOpenCover}
2348	  \uses{def:chartLocus, def:gr_rankQuotient}
2349	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
2350	  % sub-section "Construction by gluing together affine patches" (read from
2351	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L823-L828).
2352	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2353	  % "For any $J\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(J) =d$, let
2354	  %  $P^I_J = \det(X^I_J) \in \Z[X^I]$
2355	  %  where $X^I_J$ is the $J$ th minor of $X^I$.
2356	  %  Let $U^I_J = \spec \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$
2357	  %  the open subscheme of $U^I$ where $P^I_J$ is invertible. This means
2358	  %  the $d\times d$-matrix $X^I_J$ admits an inverse $(X^I_J)^{-1}$
2359	  %  on $U^I_J$."
2360	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
2361	  For a rank-\(d\) quotient \(x = \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) of \(\mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}\)
2362	  on \(T\) (\cref{def:gr_rankQuotient}), the family of chart loci
2363	  \(\{\,T_I = \mathrm{chartLocus}(x, I)\,\}_{\#(I) = d}\) (\cref{def:chartLocus}) is an open
2364	  cover of \(T\): \(\bigcup_{\#(I)=d} T_I = T\).
2365	\end{lemma}
2366	\begin{proof}
2367	  \uses{def:chartLocus, def:gr_rankQuotient, def:isoLocus, def:gr_matrixEndRect,
2368	    lem:gr_exists_rightInverse_of_epi_matrixEndRect, lem:gr_exists_isUnit_submatrix}
2369	  Each \(T_I\) is open, being an iso-locus (\cref{def:isoLocus}). It remains to show the
2370	  \(T_I\) cover \(T\), i.e.\ that every point \(t \in T\) lies in some \(T_I\). Fix \(t\) and
2371	  choose an \emph{affine} open \(W \ni t\) over which \(\mathcal{F}\) is trivialised,
2372	  \(\mathcal{F}|_W \cong \mathcal{O}_W^{\,d}\) (such \(W\) exists because \(\mathcal{F}\) is
2373	  locally free of rank \(d\)); over \(W\) the quotient \(q\) is presented by a \(d \times r\)
2374	  matrix \(M\) of sections of \(\mathcal{O}_W\), i.e.\ \(q|_W\) is conjugate to
2375	  \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(M)\) (\cref{def:gr_matrixEndRect}).
2376	
2377	  \emph{Global splitting over \(W\).} Because \(q\) is an epimorphism
2378	  (\cref{def:gr_rankQuotient}), so is the presenting homomorphism
2379	  \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(M)\) of free sheaves on the affine \(W\). Over an affine base such
2380	  an epimorphism of free sheaves splits -- free modules are projective, so the epimorphism
2381	  admits a section -- and reading the section off as a matrix yields a right inverse
2382	  \(G \in \operatorname{Mat}_{r\times d}(\Gamma(W,\mathcal{O}_W))\) with \(M \cdot G = 1\)
2383	  (\cref{lem:gr_exists_rightInverse_of_epi_matrixEndRect}). Thus \(q\) is globally split over
2384	  \(W\), with no recourse to stalks or Nakayama propagation.
2385	
2386	  \emph{Choosing the index set at \(t\).} Reduce the identity \(M \cdot G = 1\) modulo the
2387	  maximal ideal of the local ring at \(t\): writing \(\kappa(t)\) for the residue field, the
2388	  images \(\bar M, \bar G\) over \(\kappa(t)\) still satisfy \(\bar M \cdot \bar G = 1\), so
2389	  \(\bar M\) is a right-invertible \(d \times r\) matrix over the field \(\kappa(t)\). A
2390	  right-invertible matrix over a field has an invertible \(d\)-column minor: its columns span
2391	  \(\kappa(t)^{d}\), a spanning family contains a basis, and the corresponding \(d\) columns
2392	  form an invertible \(d \times d\) submatrix (\cref{lem:gr_exists_isUnit_submatrix}). Pick
2393	  such a size-\(d\) index set \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\).
2394	
2395	  \emph{Cutting out the basic open.} Let \(P_I = \det(M_I)\) be the determinant of the
2396	  \(I\)-indexed \(d \times d\) minor of \(M\). By the previous step its residue at \(t\) is
2397	  nonzero, so \(P_I\) is a unit at \(t\); hence \(t\) lies in the basic open
2398	  \(W_{P_I} = \{P_I \ne 0\} \subseteq W\) where \(P_I\) is invertible. On \(W_{P_I}\) the
2399	  minor matrix \(M_I\) is invertible, so the chart composite \(s_I \circ q\) -- conjugate
2400	  there to \(\mathrm{matrixEndRect}(M_I)\) -- restricts to an isomorphism, i.e.\
2401	  \(W_{P_I} \subseteq \mathrm{isoLocus}(s_I \circ q) = T_I\) (\cref{def:chartLocus}).
2402	  Therefore \(t \in T_I\), and the chart loci cover \(T\).
2403	\end{proof}

2405	\begin{definition}[Chart matrix of a rank-\(d\) quotient]
2406	  \label{def:gr_chartMatrix}
2407	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartMatrix}
2408	  \uses{def:gr_chartComposite, def:chartLocus, lem:isIso_pullback_isoLocus_map,
2409	    lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso, def:gr_presentedMatrix}
2410	  Over the chart locus \(T_I = \mathrm{chartLocus}(x, I)\) (\cref{def:chartLocus}) the chart
2411	  composite \(s_I \circ q\) pulls back to an isomorphism
2412	  (\cref{lem:isIso_pullback_isoLocus_map}). Trivialising \(\mathcal{F}|_{T_I}\) by its
2413	  inverse presents the restricted quotient as the conjugated free-sheaf homomorphism
2414	  \[
2415	    Q_r^{-1} \circ \iota_{T_I}^{*}q \circ (\iota_{T_I}^{*}(s_I \circ q))^{-1} \circ Q_d
2416	      \;:\; \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,r} \longrightarrow \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,d},
2417	  \]
2418	  with \(Q_r, Q_d\) the free-pullback comparisons (\cref{lem:gr_pullbackFreeIso}). The
2419	  \emph{chart matrix} \(M^I = \mathrm{chartMatrix}(x, I) \in
2420	  \operatorname{Mat}_{d\times r}(\Gamma(T_I, \mathcal{O}_{T_I}))\) reads off this
2421	  homomorphism entrywise through the section-extraction \(\mathrm{unitEndSection}\). It is
2422	  the special case of the presented matrix (\cref{def:gr_presentedMatrix}) along the
2423	  chart-locus inclusion; its \(I\)-minor is the identity \(1_{d\times d}\).
2424	\end{definition}

2426	\begin{definition}[Chart morphism of a rank-\(d\) quotient]
2427	  \label{def:gr_chartMorphism}
2428	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.chartMorphism}
2429	  \uses{def:gr_chartMatrix, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix}
2430	  The \emph{chart morphism} \(\varphi_I : T_I \to U^I\) into the affine chart
2431	  \(U^I = \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) is the morphism classified,
2432	  through the \(\Gamma\)--\(\Spec\) adjunction, by the \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra homomorphism
2433	  \(R^I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to \Gamma(T_I, \mathcal{O}_{T_I})\) sending each variable
2434	  \(x^I_{p,q}\) of the universal matrix \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}) to the
2435	  corresponding entry of the chart matrix \(M^I\) (\cref{def:gr_chartMatrix}). By
2436	  construction \(\varphi_I^{*}X^I = M^I\), and hence \(\varphi_I^{*}u^I = q|_{T_I}\) in the
2437	  \(I\)-trivialisation.
2438	\end{definition}

2440	\subsection{Overlap compatibility of the chart morphisms}
2441	
2442	On the intersection \(P = T_I \times_T T_J\) of two chart loci both chart composites pull
2443	back to isomorphisms, so the pulled-back quotient is presented by both chart matrices. The
2444	blocks below assemble the transports comparing the two presentations and proving the two
2445	chart morphisms agree into the glued scheme: invertibility along a composite
2446	(\cref{lem:gr_isIso_pullback_map_comp}), the presented matrix along a composite
2447	(\cref{lem:gr_presentedMatrix_comp}) and under an equality of base morphisms
2448	(\cref{lem:gr_presentedMatrix_congr}), the chart matrix as a presented matrix
2449	(\cref{lem:gr_chartMatrix_eq_presentedMatrix}), its own minor
2450	(\cref{lem:gr_presentedMatrix_submatrix_self}), the realisation
2451	\(\mathrm{aeval}(M^I)(X^I) = M^I\) (\cref{lem:gr_universalMatrix_map_presentedMatrix}), the
2452	image-matrix transport (\cref{lem:gr_imageMatrix_map_ringHom}), chart-morphism naturality
2453	(\cref{lem:gr_comp_chartMorphism}), the glue-condition identity
2454	(\cref{lem:gr_chart_point_eq}), and finally the overlap compatibility itself
2455	(\cref{lem:gr_chartMorphism_glue_compat}).

2457	\begin{lemma}[Pullback-invertibility persists along precomposition]
2458	  \label{lem:gr_isIso_pullback_map_comp}
2459	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isIso_pullback_map_comp}
2460	  \uses{def:modules_pullbackComp}
2461	  For composable scheme morphisms \(p : W \to V\), \(a : V \to X\) and a morphism
2462	  \(c : \mathcal{M} \to \mathcal{N}\) of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules: if the
2463	  pullback \(a^{*}c\) is an isomorphism, so is \((p \circ a)^{*}c\).

2465	\begin{proof}
2466	  \uses{def:modules_pullbackComp}

2469	  identifies \((p \circ a)^{*}c\) with \(p^{*}(a^{*}c)\), which is invertible.

2764	
2765	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) represents the Grassmannian functor]
2766	  \label{thm:grassmannian_universal_property}
2767	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.represents}
2768	  \uses{def:grassmannian_functor, def:tautological_quotient,
2769	    def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_affine_chart,
2770	    def:gr_universal_matrix, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle}
2771	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
2772	  % Exercise (2) "Grassmannian as a Quot scheme" (read from
2773	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L546-L566).
2774	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2775	  % "Show that $\grass(r,d)$ together with the quotient
2776	  %  $u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U$ represents the
2777	  %  contravariant functor
2778	  %  $$\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$$
2779	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any $T$ the
2780	  %  set of all equivalence classes
2781	  %  $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
2782	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$.
2783	  %  Therefore, $\quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}$
2784	  %  exists, and equals $\grass(r,d)$."
2785	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2).}
2786	  The Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}),
2787	  together with the tautological quotient \(\langle \mathcal{U}, u \rangle\)
2788	  (\cref{def:tautological_quotient}), represents the functor
2789	  \(\operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:grassmannian_functor}). That is, for
2790	  every scheme \(T\) the natural map
2791	  \[
2792	    \operatorname{Hom}(T, \mathrm{Gr}(r,d))
2793	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
2794	    \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T),
2795	    \qquad
2796	    \bigl(\,\varphi : T \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\,\bigr)
2797	      \;\longmapsto\;
2798	    \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U},\, \varphi^{*}u \rangle,
2799	  \]
2800	  is a bijection, natural in \(T\). Consequently the Quot scheme
2801	  \(\quot^{\,d,\,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^{r}\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}/
2802	  \mathbb{Z}/\mathbb{Z}}\) of rank-\(d\) quotients of the trivial rank-\(r\) sheaf
2803	  exists and equals \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\); the tautological quotient is the
2804	  universal family.
2805	\end{theorem}
2806	
2807	\begin{proof}
2808	  \uses{def:tautological_quotient, def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf,
2809	    def:gr_chart_quotient, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_universal_matrix,
2810	    def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle, def:gr_glued_scheme,
2811	    def:grassmannian_functor, lem:tautologicalQuotient_epi,
2812	    def:grPointOfRankQuotient, lem:chartLocus_isOpenCover, def:chartLocus,

2816	    lem:gr_chartLocus_rqPullback, lem:gr_chartMorphism_glue_compat}
2817	  Naturality of \(\varphi \mapsto \langle \varphi^{*}\mathcal{U}, \varphi^{*}u
2818	  \rangle\) is immediate: pullback is functorial and \(\varphi^{*}u\) is again a
2819	  surjection onto a rank-\(d\) locally free sheaf, so the assignment defines a
2820	  morphism of functors \(\operatorname{Hom}(-,\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)) \to
2821	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)\). It remains to show it is a bijection for each
2822	  \(T\); we exhibit an inverse, constructed Zariski-locally on \(T\) and glued.
2823	
2824	  \emph{From a quotient to a morphism.} Let \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q \rangle \in
2825	  \operatorname{Grass}(r,d)(T)\), so \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r} \twoheadrightarrow
2826	  \mathcal{F}\) with \(\mathcal{F}\) locally free of rank \(d\). For a size-\(d\)
2827	  subset \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) let \(T_I \subseteq T\) be the locus where
2828	  the composite
2829	  \[
2830	    \mathcal{O}_T^{\,d}
2831	      \overset{\ \iota_I\ }{\hookrightarrow} \mathcal{O}_T^{\,r}
2832	      \xrightarrow{\ q\ } \mathcal{F},
2833	  \]
2834	  with \(\iota_I\) the inclusion of the \(I\)-indexed coordinates, is an
2835	  isomorphism. This locus is open: in a local trivialisation of \(\mathcal{F}\)
2836	  the composite is a \(d \times d\) matrix and \(T_I\) is the non-vanishing locus
2837	  of its determinant. The \(T_I\) cover \(T\): at any point \(t\), the
2838	  \(d\)-dimensional fibre quotient \(q(t) : \kappa(t)^{r} \twoheadrightarrow
2839	  \mathcal{F}\otimes\kappa(t)\) is surjective, so some \(d\) of the \(r\) standard
2840	  basis vectors map to a basis of the \(d\)-dimensional fibre, i.e.\ \(t \in T_I\)
2841	  for that \(I\) (by Nakayama the isomorphism then holds in a neighbourhood).
2842	
2843	  On \(T_I\), trivialise \(\mathcal{F}\) using \(\iota_I^{*}\): the quotient
2844	  becomes a surjection \(q_I : \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,r} \to \mathcal{O}_{T_I}^{\,d}\)
2845	  represented by a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M^I\) whose \(I\)-th minor \(M^I_I\) is
2846	  the identity \(1_{d \times d}\) -- exactly the shape of the universal matrix
2847	  \(X^I\) (\cref{def:gr_universal_matrix}). The \(d(r-d)\) free entries of \(M^I\)
2848	  are global functions on \(T_I\) and hence determine a unique
2849	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra homomorphism \(R^I = \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to
2850	  \Gamma(T_I, \mathcal{O}_{T_I})\) sending \(x^I_{p,q}\) to the corresponding entry
2851	  of \(M^I\), i.e.\ a unique morphism \(\varphi_I : T_I \to U^I\)
2852	  (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) with \(\varphi_I^{*}X^I = M^I\) and thus
2853	  \(\varphi_I^{*}u^I = q_I\) (\cref{def:gr_chart_quotient}).
2854	
2855	  \emph{Gluing.} On the overlap \(T_I \cap T_J\) the two trivialisations differ by
2856	  the change-of-basis \((M^I_J)^{-1}\), which is exactly the bundle transition
2857	  \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) of \(\mathcal{U}\)
2858	  (\cref{def:gr_universal_quotient_sheaf}); concretely
2859	  \(M^J = (M^I_J)^{-1} M^I = \varphi_I^{*}\theta_{I,J}(X^J)\)
